category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper/Part A - General Aptitude

info.text:
CSIR-UGC NET Mathematical Sciences — Model Question Paper

Part A: General Aptitude (Q1–15). Marks: +2 for correct, −0.5 for incorrect. Attempt any 12 out of 15.

name.text: MODEL Q1 - Net percentage change after successive changes

questiontext.text: A number is first increased by 20% and then decreased by 20%. The net percentage change in the number is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 4% decrease

Let original number = 100. After 20% increase: 120. After 20% decrease: \(120 \times 0.80 = 96\). Net change = \(-4\%\).

name.text: MODEL Q2 - Train speed calculation

questiontext.text: A train 150 m long passes a stationary pole in 15 seconds. The speed of the train in km/hr is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 36 km/hr

Speed \(= \dfrac{150}{15} = 10\) m/s \(= 10 \times \dfrac{18}{5} = 36\) km/hr.

name.text: MODEL Q3 - Find n from sum of natural numbers

questiontext.text: The sum of the first \(n\) natural numbers is 630. The value of \(n\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 35

\(\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = 630 \Rightarrow n(n+1) = 1260\). Checking: \(35 \times 36 = 1260\). So \(n = 35\).

name.text: MODEL Q4 - Logic puzzle: who is guilty

questiontext.text: Among four people P, Q, R, S, exactly one committed a mistake. P says "Q did it"; Q says "S did it"; R says "I did not do it"; S says "Q is lying". Only the guilty person lies. Who is guilty?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Q

If Q is guilty: Q lies ("S did it" is false ✓). P is truthful ("Q did it" is true ✓). R is truthful ("I did not do it" is true ✓). S is truthful ("Q is lying" is true ✓). All statements are consistent. No other person works: if P is guilty, P lies ("Q did it" is false ✓) but then Q is truthful and "S did it" must be true — contradiction.

name.text: MODEL Q5 - Next term in sequence

questiontext.text: Find the next term in the sequence: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, __?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 42

The general term is \(n(n+1)\): \(1\times2=2,\ 2\times3=6,\ 3\times4=12,\ 4\times5=20,\ 5\times6=30,\ 6\times7=42\).

name.text: MODEL Q6 - Fraction of C given A and B relationships

questiontext.text: A is \(\dfrac{3}{5}\) of B, and B is \(\dfrac{2}{3}\) of C. What fraction of C is A?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) \(\dfrac{2}{5}\)

\(A = \dfrac{3}{5}B = \dfrac{3}{5} \times \dfrac{2}{3}C = \dfrac{2}{5}C\).

name.text: MODEL Q7 - Simple interest rate for doubling

questiontext.text: A sum of money doubles itself in 8 years at simple interest. The annual rate of interest (in %) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 12.5%

\(\text{SI} = P \Rightarrow \dfrac{P \times r \times 8}{100} = P \Rightarrow r = \dfrac{100}{8} = 12.5\%\).

name.text: MODEL Q8 - Time-work: B finishes remaining work

questiontext.text: A can complete a work in 12 days and B in 18 days. They work together for 4 days, after which A leaves. How many more days does B need to finish the remaining work?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 8 days

Combined rate \(= \dfrac{1}{12}+\dfrac{1}{18} = \dfrac{5}{36}\) per day. Work in 4 days \(= \dfrac{20}{36} = \dfrac{5}{9}\). Remaining \(= \dfrac{4}{9}\). B's rate \(= \dfrac{1}{18}\). Days \(= \dfrac{4}{9} \div \dfrac{1}{18} = 8\) days.

name.text: MODEL Q9 - Cost of fencing a circular field

questiontext.text: A circular field has radius 14 m. The cost of fencing it at the rate of Rs. 5 per metre is (use \(\pi = \frac{22}{7}\))

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Rs. 440

Circumference \(= 2\pi r = 2 \times \dfrac{22}{7} \times 14 = 88\) m. Cost \(= 88 \times 5 = \text{Rs. }440\).

name.text: MODEL Q10 - Find sixth number from average

questiontext.text: The average of 5 numbers is 24. When a sixth number is included, the new average becomes 22. What is the sixth number?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 12

Sum of 5 numbers \(= 5 \times 24 = 120\). Sum of 6 numbers \(= 6 \times 22 = 132\). Sixth number \(= 132 - 120 = 12\).

name.text: MODEL Q11 - Distance from starting point (direction sense)

questiontext.text: A man walks 4 km North, then turns right and walks 3 km East. How far (in km) is he from his starting point?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 5 km

The displacement forms a right triangle with legs 4 km and 3 km. Distance \(= \sqrt{4^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5\) km.

name.text: MODEL Q12 - Coding-decoding: MANGO in code

questiontext.text: In a certain code, APPLE is written as BQQMF (each letter shifted forward by one). How is MANGO written in the same code?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) NBOHP

Shift each letter forward by 1: M→N, A→B, N→O, G→H, O→P. So MANGO → NBOHP.

name.text: MODEL Q13 - Blood relations: P is related to S as

questiontext.text: P is the brother of Q. Q is the sister of R. R is the father of S. How is P related to S?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Uncle

P and Q are siblings. Q is the sister of R, so P, Q and R are siblings. R is S's father. Therefore P is S's uncle (father's brother).

name.text: MODEL Q14 - Which is NOT a prime number

questiontext.text: Which of the following is NOT a prime number?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 91

\(91 = 7 \times 13\), so 91 is composite. The others — 83, 89, 97 — are all prime.

name.text: MODEL Q15 - Number of students playing neither sport

questiontext.text: In a class of 60 students, 40 play cricket, 30 play football, and 10 play both. How many students play neither sport?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 0

\(|C \cup F| = 40 + 30 - 10 = 60\). Since the class has exactly 60 students, all of them play at least one sport. Number playing neither \(= 60 - 60 = 0\).

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper/Part B - Core Mathematics

info.text: Part B: Core Mathematics (Q16–40). Marks: +3 for correct, −0.75 for incorrect. Attempt any 20 out of 25.

name.text: MODEL Q16 - Convergence of sequence (1+1/n)^n

questiontext.text: Consider the sequence \(a_n = \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{n}\right)^n\) for \(n \geq 1\). Then

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) \(\{a_n\}\) is strictly increasing and converges to \(e\)

By AM-GM inequality one shows \(a_n < a_{n+1}\), and by the Binomial Theorem the limit is \(e \approx 2.718\ldots\)

answer.text: \(\{a_n\}\) is strictly increasing and converges to \(e\)

name.text: MODEL Q17 - Convergence of series 1/(n log n)

questiontext.text: The series \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n \log n}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Diverges

By the integral test, \(\displaystyle\int_2^\infty \dfrac{dx}{x\log x} = \Big[\log(\log x)\Big]_2^\infty = +\infty\). Hence the series diverges.

name.text: MODEL Q18 - Continuous additive function on R

questiontext.text: Let \(f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) be a continuous function satisfying \(f(x+y) = f(x) + f(y)\) for all \(x, y \in \mathbb{R}\). Then \(f\) must be of the form

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) \(f(x) = cx\) for some constant \(c \in \mathbb{R}\)

From \(f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\) one deduces \(f(nx)=nf(x)\) for integers, then rationals, and continuity extends this to all reals: \(f(x)=f(1)\cdot x\). Set \(c=f(1)\).

name.text: MODEL Q19 - Bound on differentiable f with |f'|≤1 and f(0)=0

questiontext.text: Let \(f: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}\) be differentiable with \(|f'(x)| \leq 1\) for all \(x\) and \(f(0) = 0\). Then

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) \(|f(x)| \leq x\) for all \(x \in [0,1]\)

By the Mean Value Theorem, \(|f(x) - f(0)| = |f'(c)| \cdot |x - 0| \leq 1 \cdot x = x\).

name.text: MODEL Q20 - Value of Beta integral x^n(1-x)

questiontext.text: The value of the integral \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 x^n(1-x)\,dx\) (for integer \(n \geq 0\)) equals

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(\dfrac{1}{(n+1)(n+2)}\)

Using the Beta function: \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 x^n(1-x)^1\,dx = B(n+1,2) = \dfrac{n!\,1!}{(n+2)!} = \dfrac{1}{(n+1)(n+2)}\).

name.text: MODEL Q21 - Intersection of subspaces when dim sum exceeds n

questiontext.text: Let \(V\) be a vector space of dimension \(n\) over \(\mathbb{R}\). If \(W_1\) and \(W_2\) are subspaces with \(\dim W_1 + \dim W_2 > n\), then

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) \(W_1 \cap W_2 \neq \{0\}\)

By the dimension formula: \(\dim(W_1 \cap W_2) = \dim W_1 + \dim W_2 - \dim(W_1+W_2) \geq \dim W_1 + \dim W_2 - n > 0\). Hence the intersection is non-trivial.

name.text: MODEL Q22 - Eigenvalues of rotation matrix

questiontext.text: The eigenvalues of the matrix \(A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\) over \(\mathbb{C}\) are

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) \(\pm i\)

Characteristic polynomial: \(\det(A - \lambda I) = \lambda^2 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \pm i\). There are no real eigenvalues.

name.text: MODEL Q23 - Determinant of I + all-ones matrix

questiontext.text: Let \(J\) denote the \(n \times n\) matrix with all entries equal to 1, and let \(A = I + J\) (where \(I\) is the identity matrix). Then \(\det(A)\) equals

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) \(n+1\)

Eigenvalues of \(J\): \(n\) (eigenvector \(\mathbf{1}\)) and \(0\) (multiplicity \(n-1\)). Eigenvalues of \(A = I+J\): \(n+1\) and \(1\) (\(n-1\) times). \(\det(A) = (n+1) \cdot 1^{n-1} = n+1\).

name.text: MODEL Q24 - Rank from nullity (rank-nullity theorem)

questiontext.text: A linear map \(T: \mathbb{R}^5 \to \mathbb{R}^3\) has nullity 3. The rank of \(T\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 2

By the Rank-Nullity Theorem: \(\text{rank}(T) + \text{nullity}(T) = \dim(\mathbb{R}^5) = 5\). Hence \(\text{rank}(T) = 5 - 3 = 2\).

name.text: MODEL Q25 - Eigenvalues of real symmetric matrix

questiontext.text: All eigenvalues of a real symmetric matrix are

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Real

If \(A\) is real symmetric and \(Av = \lambda v\) (\(v \neq 0\)), then \(\bar{\lambda}\|v\|^2 = (Av,v) = (v,Av) = \lambda\|v\|^2\), so \(\lambda = \bar{\lambda}\), i.e., \(\lambda \in \mathbb{R}\).

name.text: MODEL Q26 - Order of permutation (1 2 3)(4 5) in S5

questiontext.text: The order of the permutation \((1\;2\;3)(4\;5)\) in the symmetric group \(S_5\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) 6

The permutation is a product of disjoint cycles of lengths 3 and 2. The order equals \(\text{lcm}(3, 2) = 6\).

name.text: MODEL Q27 - Group of order 35 is cyclic

questiontext.text: A group of order 35 is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Cyclic

\(35 = 5 \times 7\). By Sylow's theorem, the number of Sylow 5-subgroups divides 7 and is \(\equiv 1 \pmod{5}\): only possibility is 1. Similarly the Sylow 7-subgroup is unique. Both are normal, so the group is \(\mathbb{Z}_5 \times \mathbb{Z}_7 \cong \mathbb{Z}_{35}\), which is cyclic.

name.text: MODEL Q28 - Ideal (x,2) in Z[x]

questiontext.text: In the polynomial ring \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\), the ideal \((x, 2)\) generated by \(x\) and \(2\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) A maximal ideal

\(\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x,2) \cong \mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z} = \mathbb{F}_2\), which is a field. An ideal is maximal if and only if the quotient ring is a field, so \((x,2)\) is maximal. It is not principal since \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\) is not a PID.

answer.text: Equal to the ideal generated by \(2x\)

name.text: MODEL Q29 - Where is |z|^2 analytic

questiontext.text: The function \(f(z) = |z|^2\) (as a function of a complex variable \(z\)) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Analytic only at \(z = 0\)

Write \(f(x+iy) = x^2+y^2\). The Cauchy–Riemann equations \(u_x = v_y,\; u_y = -v_x\) with \(u=x^2+y^2,\, v=0\) give \(2x=0,\; 2y=0\). So CR equations hold only at \(z=0\).

name.text: MODEL Q30 - Contour integral dz/(z^2-1) over |z|=2

questiontext.text: The value of \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=2} \dfrac{dz}{z^2-1}\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 0

Poles at \(z = \pm 1\), both inside \(|z|=2\). Partial fractions: \(\dfrac{1}{z^2-1} = \dfrac{1}{2(z-1)} - \dfrac{1}{2(z+1)}\). Residue at \(z=1\): \(\tfrac{1}{2}\); at \(z=-1\): \(-\tfrac{1}{2}\). Sum of residues \(= 0\). By the Residue Theorem: integral \(= 2\pi i \cdot 0 = 0\).

name.text: MODEL Q31 - Compact subsets of R (Heine-Borel)

questiontext.text: A subset \(K\) of \(\mathbb{R}\) (with the standard metric) is compact if and only if \(K\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Closed and bounded

This is the Heine–Borel theorem for subsets of \(\mathbb{R}^n\): compactness is equivalent to being closed and bounded.

name.text: MODEL Q32 - Connectedness of R^2 minus origin

questiontext.text: The space \(\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{(0,0)\}\) (the plane minus the origin) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Path-connected

Given any two points \(p, q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{0\}\), one can connect them by a path avoiding the origin (e.g., go around it). Hence the space is path-connected (and therefore connected).

name.text: MODEL Q33 - General solution of y'' - 3y' + 2y = 0

questiontext.text: The general solution of the ODE \(y'' - 3y' + 2y = 0\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) \(y = C_1 e^x + C_2 e^{2x}\)

Characteristic equation: \(r^2 - 3r + 2 = (r-1)(r-2) = 0\). Roots: \(r = 1, 2\). General solution: \(y = C_1 e^x + C_2 e^{2x}\).

name.text: MODEL Q34 - Classification of u_t = u_xx

questiontext.text: The partial differential equation \(u_t = u_{xx}\) is called the

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Heat equation

The PDE \(u_t = u_{xx}\) is the one-dimensional heat (diffusion) equation, which is parabolic. The wave equation is \(u_{tt} = c^2 u_{xx}\); Laplace's equation is \(u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0\).

name.text: MODEL Q35 - Solution blow-up: y' = y^2, y(0)=1

questiontext.text: The unique solution of the initial value problem \(y' = y^2,\; y(0) = 1\) is valid on the interval

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) \((-\infty,\, 1)\)

Separating variables: \(\displaystyle\int \dfrac{dy}{y^2} = \int dx \Rightarrow -\dfrac{1}{y} = x + C\). With \(y(0)=1\): \(C = -1\), so \(y = \dfrac{1}{1-x}\). This blows up at \(x=1\) and is defined for \(x < 1\).

name.text: MODEL Q36 - Completeness: Cauchy sequences

questiontext.text: A metric space \((X, d)\) is said to be complete if

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Every Cauchy sequence in \(X\) converges to a point in \(X\)

This is the definition of completeness. Note that in a complete space not every sequence converges (e.g., divergent sequences exist); only Cauchy sequences are guaranteed to converge.

answer.text: Every Cauchy sequence in \(X\) converges to a point in \(X\)

name.text: MODEL Q37 - Variance of Binomial(10, 0.3)

questiontext.text: If \(X \sim \text{Binomial}(10, 0.3)\), then \(\text{Var}(X)\) equals

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 2.1

\(\text{Var}(X) = np(1-p) = 10 \times 0.3 \times 0.7 = 2.1\).

name.text: MODEL Q38 - Type of singularity of sin(z)/z at z=0

questiontext.text: The function \(f(z) = \dfrac{\sin z}{z}\) has at \(z = 0\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) A removable singularity

\(\displaystyle\lim_{z\to 0}\dfrac{\sin z}{z} = 1\). Since the limit exists and is finite, \(z=0\) is a removable singularity. Defining \(f(0)=1\) makes \(f\) entire.

name.text: MODEL Q39 - Lebesgue measure of Cantor set

questiontext.text: The Lebesgue measure of the (middle-thirds) Cantor set \(\mathcal{C} \subset [0,1]\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 0

At step \(n\), the total measure removed is \(\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \dfrac{2^k}{3^{k+1}} \to 1\) as \(n \to \infty\). Hence \(\mu(\mathcal{C}) = 1 - 1 = 0\). Despite having measure zero, \(\mathcal{C}\) is uncountable.

name.text: MODEL Q40 - Tower Law for field extensions

questiontext.text: Let \(F \subseteq E \subseteq K\) be field extensions with \([K:F] = 6\) and \([E:F] = 2\). Then \([K:E]\) equals

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 3

By the Tower Law (multiplicativity of degrees): \([K:F] = [K:E]\cdot[E:F]\). Hence \(6 = [K:E] \times 2\), so \([K:E] = 3\).

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper/Part C - Advanced Mathematics (MSQ)

info.text: Part C: Advanced Mathematics — Multiple Select Questions (Q41–55). Marks: +4.75 for fully correct selection, no negative marking. Attempt any 12 out of 15. Select ALL correct options.

name.text: MODEL Q41 - Properties of uniformly continuous functions (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Which of the following statements are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) Every Lipschitz function on \(\mathbb{R}\) is uniformly continuous
(2) Every uniformly continuous function on \(\mathbb{R}\) is Lipschitz
(3) A continuous function on a compact set is uniformly continuous
(4) A uniformly continuous function on a bounded open interval \((a,b)\) can be continuously extended to \([a,b]\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (3), (4)

(1) TRUE: Lipschitz \(\Rightarrow\) uniform continuity directly from definition.
(2) FALSE: \(f(x)=\sqrt{x}\) on \([0,\infty)\) is uniformly continuous but not Lipschitz at 0.
(3) TRUE: Heine–Cantor theorem.
(4) TRUE: Uniform continuity on \((a,b)\) means the function is Cauchy-continuous; it extends uniquely to the endpoints.

answer.text: Every Lipschitz function on \(\mathbb{R}\) is uniformly continuous
Every uniformly continuous function on \(\mathbb{R}\) is Lipschitz
A continuous function on a compact set is uniformly continuous
A uniformly continuous function on a bounded open interval \((a,b)\) can be continuously extended to \([a,b]\)

name.text: MODEL Q42 - Properties of matrices (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Let \(A\) be an \(n \times n\) real matrix. Which of the following are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) If \(A^2 = A\), then \(A\) is diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{R}\)
(2) If \(A^2 = 0\), then \(A = 0\)
(3) If \(A\) is orthogonal, then all its complex eigenvalues have modulus 1
(4) \(\text{tr}(AB) = \text{tr}(BA)\) for any \(n \times n\) matrix \(B\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (3), (4)

(1) TRUE: An idempotent matrix satisfies \(p(\lambda)=\lambda(\lambda-1)=0\), so it has minimal polynomial dividing \(\lambda(\lambda-1)\), which has distinct roots — hence diagonalizable.
(2) FALSE: The matrix \(\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\) satisfies \(A^2=0\) but \(A\neq 0\).
(3) TRUE: For orthogonal \(A\), \(\|Av\|=\|v\|\), so \(|\lambda|=1\).
(4) TRUE: \(\text{tr}(AB) = \sum_{i,j}a_{ij}b_{ji} = \text{tr}(BA)\).

answer.text: If \(A^2 = A\), then \(A\) is diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{R}\)
If \(A\) is orthogonal, then all its complex eigenvalues have modulus 1

name.text: MODEL Q43 - Which groups are abelian (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Which of the following are abelian groups? (Select all correct options)

(1) \(S_3\) (symmetric group on 3 letters)
(2) \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) for any \(n \geq 1\)
(3) Every group of order \(p^2\) (\(p\) prime)
(4) Every group of order 6

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (3)

(1) FALSE: \(S_3\) is non-abelian (e.g., \((12)(13) \neq (13)(12)\)).
(2) TRUE: \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) is cyclic, hence abelian.
(3) TRUE: A group of order \(p^2\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}_{p^2}\) or \(\mathbb{Z}_p \times \mathbb{Z}_p\), both abelian.
(4) FALSE: \(S_3\) has order 6 and is non-abelian.

name.text: MODEL Q44 - Properties of entire functions (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Let \(f: \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}\) be an entire function. Which of the following are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) If \(f\) is bounded, then \(f\) is constant
(2) If \(|f(z)| \to \infty\) as \(|z| \to \infty\), then \(f\) is a polynomial
(3) If \(f\) is injective, then \(f(z) = az + b\) for some \(a \neq 0,\, b \in \mathbb{C}\)
(4) If \(f\) maps \(\mathbb{R}\) into \(\mathbb{R}\) and is non-constant, then \(f\) is a polynomial

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3)

(1) TRUE: Liouville's theorem.
(2) TRUE: \(\infty\) is a pole (not essential) so \(f\) is a polynomial by the structure theorem for entire functions with polynomial growth.
(3) TRUE: An injective entire function has at most a simple zero of \(f'\) nowhere and can be shown to be linear by Picard / open mapping arguments.
(4) FALSE: \(f(z) = \sin z\) maps \(\mathbb{R}\) into \(\mathbb{R}\) and is entire but not a polynomial.

answer.text: If \(f\) is bounded, then \(f\) is constant
If \(|f(z)| \to \infty\) as \(|z| \to \infty\), then \(f\) is a polynomial
If \(f\) is injective, then \(f(z) = az + b\) for some \(a \neq 0,\, b \in \mathbb{C}\)
If \(f\) maps \(\mathbb{R}\) into \(\mathbb{R}\) and is non-constant, then \(f\) is a polynomial

name.text: MODEL Q45 - Connected topological spaces (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Which of the following topological spaces are connected? (Select all correct options)

(1) \(\mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}\)
(2) \(\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{(0,0)\}\)
(3) The Cantor set \(\mathcal{C}\)
(4) \(\text{SL}(2,\mathbb{R}) = \{A \in M_2(\mathbb{R}) : \det A = 1\}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2) and (4)

(1) FALSE: \(\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\} = (-\infty,0)\cup(0,\infty)\), a union of two disjoint open sets.
(2) TRUE: \(\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}\) is path-connected (detouring around the origin).
(3) FALSE: The Cantor set is totally disconnected (its only connected subsets are singletons).
(4) TRUE: \(\text{SL}(2,\mathbb{R})\) is a connected Lie group.

name.text: MODEL Q46 - Properties in a Hilbert space (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Let \(H\) be an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space. Which of the following are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) Every closed subspace of \(H\) has an orthogonal complement
(2) The weak topology and the norm topology on \(H\) coincide
(3) Every bounded sequence in \(H\) has a weakly convergent subsequence
(4) \(H\) is reflexive (i.e., \(H \cong H^{**}\))

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (3), (4)

(1) TRUE: Projection theorem in Hilbert spaces.
(2) FALSE: In infinite dimensions the weak topology is strictly coarser than the norm topology.
(3) TRUE: By the Banach–Alaoglu theorem, the closed unit ball is weakly compact.
(4) TRUE: Every Hilbert space is reflexive by the Riesz representation theorem.

answer.text: Every closed subspace of \(H\) has an orthogonal complement
The weak topology and norm topology on \(H\) coincide
Every bounded sequence in \(H\) has a weakly convergent subsequence

name.text: MODEL Q47 - True statements about IVP y'=y^2, y(0)=1 (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Consider the initial value problem \(y' = y^2,\; y(0) = 1\). Which of the following are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) The unique solution is \(y = \dfrac{1}{1-x}\)
(2) The solution exists for all \(x \in \mathbb{R}\)
(3) The solution blows up in finite time
(4) There exist multiple solutions satisfying the initial condition

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (3)

Separating variables yields \(y = \dfrac{1}{1-x}\), defined on \((-\infty, 1)\). This blows up at \(x=1\) (finite time blow-up). Uniqueness follows from the Picard–Lindelöf theorem since \(f(x,y)=y^2\) is locally Lipschitz. So (1) and (3) are true; (2) and (4) are false.

answer.text: The solution blows up in finite time
There exist multiple solutions satisfying the initial condition

name.text: MODEL Q48 - UFDs and PIDs in commutative algebra (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Which of the following are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\) is a UFD
(2) \(\mathbb{Z}[i]\) (Gaussian integers) is a PID
(3) \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{2}]\) is a Euclidean domain
(4) Every PID is a UFD

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (2), (3), (4)

(1) FALSE: In \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\), we have \(6 = 2 \times 3 = (1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})\), giving non-unique factorisation into irreducibles.
(2) TRUE: \(\mathbb{Z}[i]\) is a Euclidean domain with norm \(N(a+bi)=a^2+b^2\), hence a PID.
(3) TRUE: The norm \(N(a+b\sqrt{2})=|a^2-2b^2|\) makes \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{2}]\) Euclidean.
(4) TRUE: Every PID is a UFD (standard theorem in commutative algebra).

name.text: MODEL Q49 - Riemann vs Lebesgue integrability (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Which of the following are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) Every continuous function on \([0,1]\) is Riemann integrable
(2) Every Riemann integrable function on \([0,1]\) is Lebesgue integrable
(3) Every Lebesgue integrable function on \([0,1]\) is Riemann integrable
(4) The Cantor set has Lebesgue measure zero

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (4)

(1) TRUE: Continuous functions on compact intervals are Riemann integrable.
(2) TRUE: Riemann integrable functions are bounded and their discontinuities form a null set; Lebesgue integrability follows with equal values.
(3) FALSE: The Dirichlet function \(\mathbf{1}_{\mathbb{Q}}\) is Lebesgue integrable (integral 0) but not Riemann integrable.
(4) TRUE: The middle-thirds Cantor set is constructed by removing open intervals of total length 1.

answer.text: Every continuous function on \([0,1]\) is Riemann integrable
Every Riemann integrable function on \([0,1]\) is Lebesgue integrable
Every Lebesgue integrable function on \([0,1]\) is Riemann integrable
The Cantor set has Lebesgue measure zero

name.text: MODEL Q50 - Properties of normal matrices (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
For an \(n\times n\) complex matrix \(A\), which of the following are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) \(A\) has at least one eigenvalue in \(\mathbb{C}\)
(2) Every \(A\) is diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{C}\)
(3) If \(A\) is normal (\(AA^* = A^*A\)), then \(A\) is unitarily diagonalizable
(4) The characteristic polynomial of \(A\) has degree \(n\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (3), (4)

(1) TRUE: \(\mathbb{C}\) is algebraically closed, so every polynomial has a root.
(2) FALSE: A Jordan block \(\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\) is not diagonalizable.
(3) TRUE: The Spectral Theorem for normal matrices guarantees unitary diagonalization.
(4) TRUE: \(\det(\lambda I - A)\) is a monic polynomial of degree \(n\).

answer.text: \(A\) has at least one eigenvalue in \(\mathbb{C}\)
Every \(n\times n\) complex matrix is diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{C}\)
If \(A\) is normal, then \(A\) is unitarily diagonalizable
The characteristic polynomial of \(A\) has degree \(n\)

name.text: MODEL Q51 - Distributions of normal random variables (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be independent standard normal random variables. Which of the following are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) \(X + Y \sim N(0,\, 2)\)
(2) \(X - Y \sim N(0,\, 2)\)
(3) \(X^2 + Y^2 \sim \chi^2(2)\)
(4) \(XY\) follows a normal distribution

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3)

(1) TRUE: \(\text{Var}(X+Y)=1+1=2\), so \(X+Y\sim N(0,2)\).
(2) TRUE: \(\text{Var}(X-Y)=1+1=2\), so \(X-Y\sim N(0,2)\).
(3) TRUE: The sum of squares of \(k\) independent standard normals is \(\chi^2(k)\); here \(k=2\).
(4) FALSE: The product of two normals follows a distribution expressible in terms of modified Bessel functions, which is not normal.

name.text: MODEL Q52 - Laurent series in an annulus (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Let \(f\) be analytic on the annulus \(A = \{z \in \mathbb{C} : 1 < |z| < 3\}\). Which of the following are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) \(f\) has a Laurent series expansion \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} c_n z^n\) convergent on \(A\)
(2) If \(f\) is bounded on \(A\), then \(f\) extends to an analytic function on the entire disc \(|z| < 3\)
(3) The integral \(\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=2} f(z)\,dz\) can be non-zero
(4) \(f\) can be expressed as a power series \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n\) valid throughout \(A\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (3)

(1) TRUE: Every function analytic on an annulus has a unique Laurent expansion convergent there.
(2) FALSE: Riemann's removable singularity theorem applies to isolated singularities, not to annuli. For example, \(f(z) = 1/z\) is analytic on \(A\) but has a pole at the origin.
(3) TRUE: For example, \(f(z) = 1/z\) gives \(\oint_{|z|=2} dz/z = 2\pi i \neq 0\).
(4) FALSE: The Laurent series may contain infinitely many negative-power terms.

answer.text: \(f\) has a Laurent series \(\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} c_n z^n\) convergent on \(A\)
If \(f\) is bounded on \(A\), then \(f\) extends to an analytic function on \(|z| < 3\)
The integral \(\oint_{|z|=2} f(z)\,dz\) can be non-zero
\(f\) can be expressed as a power series \(\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n z^n\) valid throughout \(A\)

name.text: MODEL Q53 - Compact subsets (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Which of the following subsets are compact (in their standard topologies)? (Select all correct options)

(1) \([0, 1]\) with the standard topology on \(\mathbb{R}\)
(2) \((0, 1)\) with the standard topology on \(\mathbb{R}\)
(3) \(\{(x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 : x^2 + y^2 \leq 1\}\)
(4) \(\mathbb{Z}\) with the discrete topology

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1) and (3)

(1) TRUE: \([0,1]\) is closed and bounded in \(\mathbb{R}\), hence compact by Heine–Borel.
(2) FALSE: \((0,1)\) is bounded but not closed, hence not compact (no finite subcover for the open cover \(\{(1/n, 1)\}_{n\geq 2}\)).
(3) TRUE: The closed unit disk is closed and bounded in \(\mathbb{R}^2\).
(4) FALSE: In the discrete topology on \(\mathbb{Z}\), the cover \(\{\{n\} : n \in \mathbb{Z}\}\) has no finite subcover.

name.text: MODEL Q54 - Properties of Laplace equation u_xx + u_yy = 0 (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Consider the Laplace equation \(u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0\) on a bounded domain \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^2\). Which of the following are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) It is an elliptic PDE
(2) Solutions are called harmonic functions
(3) A non-constant harmonic function satisfies the maximum principle (it attains its maximum on the boundary)
(4) A harmonic function can be non-smooth (non-\(C^\infty\)) inside \(\Omega\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3)

(1) TRUE: The discriminant \(B^2-4AC = 0-4(1)(1) < 0\) confirms ellipticity.
(2) TRUE: By definition, solutions of \(\Delta u = 0\) are harmonic functions.
(3) TRUE: The strong maximum principle states that a non-constant harmonic function on a bounded domain attains its maximum (and minimum) on the boundary.
(4) FALSE: Harmonic functions are real-analytic (hence \(C^\infty\)) on any open set where they satisfy the equation.

answer.text: The Laplace equation is an elliptic PDE
A non-constant harmonic function attains its maximum on the boundary
A harmonic function can be non-\(C^\infty\) inside \(\Omega\)

name.text: MODEL Q55 - Field extensions: Tower law and algebraic extensions (MSQ)

questiontext.text:
Let \(F \subseteq E \subseteq K\) be field extensions. Which of the following are TRUE? (Select all correct options)

(1) \([K:F] = [K:E]\cdot[E:F]\) (Tower Law)
(2) If \([K:F] = p\) (a prime), then there are no intermediate fields strictly between \(F\) and \(K\)
(3) Every finite extension is algebraic
(4) Every algebraic extension is finite

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (1), (2), (3)

(1) TRUE: Tower Law — the degree is multiplicative in towers.
(2) TRUE: If \(F \subsetneq E \subsetneq K\), then \([K:F]=[K:E][E:F]\geq 2\cdot 2=4\), contradicting \([K:F]=p\) prime (with \(p\leq 3\)... actually more carefully: any intermediate \(E\) gives \([E:F]\mid p\), so \([E:F]=1\) or \(p\), meaning \(E=F\) or \(E=K\)).
(3) TRUE: If \([K:F]=n < \infty\), every \(\alpha\in K\) satisfies a polynomial of degree \(\leq n\) over \(F\).
(4) FALSE: \(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q}\) (algebraic closure) is algebraic but has infinite degree.

answer.text: If \([K:F] = p\) (prime), then there are no intermediate fields strictly between \(F\) and \(K\)
